question_text: The need to reform the United Nations Security Council is widely acknowledged, but one core argument is repeated more often than any other. What is the single most commonly cited reason for why the Security Council requires structural reform?
explanation: The single most frequently cited argument for Security Council reform is that its current composition — 5 permanent veto-wielding members (US, UK, France, Russia, China) — reflects the geopolitical distribution at the end of the Second World War in 1945 rather than the actual distribution of economic, demographic, and strategic power in the 21st century. Major economies like Germany and Japan, the world's most populous democracy India, and the largest Latin American state Brazil have no permanent representation, while all of Africa (54 countries, 1.4 billion people) has no permanent seat despite being the region where most Council peacekeeping agenda items are located. This 'frozen in 1945' argument appears in virtually every serious reform proposal and is the shared starting point of the G4, Ezulwini Consensus, Uniting for Consensus, and Razali Plan proposals. It is widely framed as a democratic deficit and legitimacy gap that undermines the Council's authority in the modern era.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: The Security Council building in New York is too small to accommodate the needs of modern diplomacy and requires physical expansion
The Secretary-General has too much administrative power over Council operations and needs to be curbed by new procedural rules
The permanent membership reflects the 1945 wartime victor power distribution and does not match current global economic, demographic, and strategic realities — producing a legitimacy deficit
The Council's rules of procedure are legally invalid because they were drafted before the 1969 Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties

elimination_hints.hint: Physical building size is not a serious argument for Council reform — reform debates are about composition, voting rules, and legitimacy, not physical infrastructure; the Council could meet anywhere with its current rules.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students mistake logistical questions for substantive institutional reform questions.

elimination_hints.hint: The Secretary-General does not have administrative power over the Council — the relationship runs the other way, with the Council recommending the Secretary-General's appointment; no serious reform proposal targets SG power over the Council.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invert the institutional hierarchy between Secretary-General and Security Council.

elimination_hints.hint: The 1969 Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties did not retroactively invalidate UN Charter rules of procedure — the Charter is a treaty whose validity predates and is not affected by the Vienna Convention; procedural validity is not a reform issue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invoke unrelated legal instruments to imagine technical invalidity when the actual reform debate is about political representation.

question_text: The G4 grouping is the most visible coalition pushing for permanent Security Council seats. Which four countries constitute the G4, and what is their core demand?
explanation: The G4 consists of Brazil, Germany, India, and Japan — four major powers that have together pushed since 2004-05 for simultaneous permanent Security Council seats. Each of the G4 members cross-supports the others' bids, giving the group collective diplomatic leverage. Their core demand is expansion of the Security Council through creation of six new permanent seats (with the G4 occupying four, plus two seats for Africa) and four or five additional non-permanent seats, bringing the Council to around 25 members total. The G4 proposal has generally accepted that new permanent members would not exercise the veto for an initial 15-year transition period, after which the question would be reviewed. The G4 strategy combines mutual endorsement with active diplomatic engagement across regional groups, though it faces significant opposition from the Uniting for Consensus (UfC) group led by Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, and South Korea — which argue against any new permanent seats. Support from the US has been inconsistent (supporting Japan and India individually but not the G4 as a package), Russia has been cautious, and China actively opposes Japan's bid.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: India, China, Russia, South Africa — demanding abolition of the veto power for all P5 members
Brazil, Germany, India, Japan — demanding new permanent Security Council seats through mutual endorsement, with expansion to around 25 members
India, Indonesia, Nigeria, Mexico — demanding regional rotation of permanent seats among developing countries
Brazil, Germany, India, Italy — demanding the replacement of the current P5 entirely with a new set of permanent members

elimination_hints.hint: China and Russia are already P5 members — they are not demanding permanent seats; China in particular opposes Japan's bid as a G4 member, so Russia and China cannot be in the same reform coalition as G4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse G4 with BRICS or other formations that include existing P5 members.

elimination_hints.hint: Indonesia, Nigeria and Mexico are not G4 members — G4 is Brazil, Germany, India, Japan; rotational seats are a different reform concept, not the G4 proposal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students conflate G4 with broader developing-country or emerging-power groupings.

elimination_hints.hint: Italy is actually a leader of the Uniting for Consensus (UfC) group which opposes the G4 proposal — Italy is in the anti-G4 coalition, not a G4 member; Japan is the fourth G4 member.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Italy (UfC opponent) with Japan (G4 member) in the reform coalitions.

question_text: India has been a long-standing aspirant for a permanent seat on the UN Security Council. What are the main arguments India presents to justify its claim to permanent membership?
explanation: India's case for a permanent Security Council seat rests on several interlocking arguments. First, demographic and democratic weight: India is the world's most populous country (surpassing China in 2023) and the world's largest democracy, making its exclusion from the core UN decision-making body a clear representational gap. Second, economic size: India is currently the fifth-largest economy by nominal GDP and the third-largest by purchasing power parity, projected to become the third-largest nominal economy within the decade. Third, contribution to UN peacekeeping: India is among the largest cumulative troop contributors to UN peacekeeping operations (over 250,000 personnel across 50+ missions since 1950) and has suffered heavy casualties in these deployments — making it a major stakeholder in the Council's core peace and security work. Fourth, developing world and non-aligned leadership: India has historically been a voice for the Global South and newly independent countries. Fifth, responsible nuclear power status: India argues that its restrained nuclear doctrine (no first use, credible minimum deterrent) and non-proliferation record justify inclusion despite being outside the NPT. India has also been elected to the non-permanent Council seat eight times, demonstrating broad diplomatic acceptance.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: India's claim rests solely on its nuclear weapons capability acquired in 1974 and 1998, which is the only relevant criterion for permanent Council membership
India's claim rests solely on its colonial history as a victim of British rule, which gives it moral authority over former imperial powers in the P5
India's claim is based only on its 1945 founding member status of the UN, which gives it automatic eligibility for permanent promotion

India's claim combines multiple arguments — world's largest population and democracy, fifth-largest economy, top peacekeeping troop contributor, Global South leadership, and responsible nuclear power status

elimination_hints.hint: Nuclear weapons are not the only or primary argument India makes — India actually stresses its non-proliferation behaviour and restraint alongside many other factors; reducing the case to nuclear weapons would undermine the 'responsible' framing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reduce complex diplomatic cases to a single factor when real foreign policy arguments combine multiple strands.

elimination_hints.hint: Colonial history is not a major argument in India's case — India invokes forward-looking factors like democracy, economy, peacekeeping contribution; grievance-based arguments would alienate P5 members whose support India seeks.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume foreign policy is driven by historical grievance when practical diplomacy emphasises constructive contributions.

elimination_hints.hint: Founding member status does not provide automatic promotion — all 51 founding members are equal in this respect, and many countries with founding status make no permanent seat claim; founding status is not a criterion in any reform proposal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume founding status confers special rights when it actually gives no additional claim under any reform framework.

question_text: The Ezulwini Consensus is the African Union's unified position on UN Security Council reform. What does it specifically demand, and why is it considered the most ambitious regional reform proposal?
explanation: The Ezulwini Consensus is a formal African Union common position adopted in March 2005 at the AU Executive Council meeting in Addis Ababa, taking its name from Ezulwini, Swaziland (now Eswatini), where preliminary discussions were held. It demands two permanent seats with full veto power for Africa, plus two additional non-permanent seats — bringing Africa's total representation to five seats. It is considered the most ambitious regional proposal for three reasons. First, it demands full veto power for the new permanent seats, rejecting the G4's willingness to accept veto-less permanent seats for a 15-year transition. Second, it insists that the AU itself (not individual member states) will decide who occupies Africa's permanent seats — effectively making African representation institutional rather than national. Third, the Ezulwini position was adopted by all 54 African states, giving Africa the largest single regional bloc in the UN and substantial voting leverage in any GA decision on reform. This vote-total leverage is balanced against the difficulty of reaching consensus on which specific African countries would occupy the seats — South Africa, Nigeria, Egypt, and Kenya have all been mentioned as candidates, but no consensus exists. The Ezulwini Consensus directly competes with the G4 proposal because their respective veto demands are incompatible.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Two permanent seats with full veto power plus two additional non-permanent seats for Africa, with the AU collectively deciding which specific African states occupy them — the most ambitious regional proposal because of the full veto demand
One permanent seat for South Africa as the African representative, with rotating observer status for other African states during their regional chairmanship periods
Replacement of all P5 members with African states, reflecting Africa's majority representation among peacekeeping mission host countries
Creation of a separate African Security Council under the African Union that would operate independently of the UN Security Council

elimination_hints.hint: Ezulwini demands two permanent seats, not one — the AU rejected the idea of a single African representative; the proposal includes no observer status arrangement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underestimate Africa's reform ambition by assuming a single regional representative when the AU position demands two seats.

elimination_hints.hint: Ezulwini does not demand replacement of P5 — it demands two additional permanent seats alongside the existing P5; complete P5 replacement is not in any serious reform proposal because it would require Charter amendment the P5 would veto.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine unrealistic reform scenarios that ignore the P5 amendment veto power that locks in their continued membership.

elimination_hints.hint: The African Union Peace and Security Council exists and operates within the AU, but Ezulwini is not about creating a separate alternative to the UN Security Council — it is about gaining permanent representation within the existing UN Security Council.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the AU's internal Peace and Security Council with the Ezulwini demand for representation within the UN Security Council.

question_text: The Uniting for Consensus (UfC) group — sometimes called the 'Coffee Club' — is the main opposition to the G4 proposal. Which countries lead it, and what does it actually propose as an alternative?
explanation: The Uniting for Consensus (UfC) group emerged in the 1990s as an informal gathering known as the 'Coffee Club' and was formalised as UfC in 2005 after the G4 formalised its proposal. Its core members and leaders are Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, South Korea, Spain, Mexico, Turkey, and several others — countries linked by shared opposition to specific G4 rivals (Italy vs Germany, Pakistan vs India, Argentina/Mexico vs Brazil, South Korea vs Japan). The UfC specifically opposes the creation of any new permanent seats, arguing that permanent membership is inherently undemocratic. Instead, it proposes: expansion of only the non-permanent member category from 10 to 20 seats (total Council size of 25); longer-term non-permanent seats (possibly immediate re-election) to allow major powers more sustained engagement; equitable geographical distribution of new non-permanent seats. This provides a middle path between the status quo and the G4's permanent-seat demand, while specifically denying the G4 members the status they seek. The G4 and UfC proposals are fundamentally incompatible — neither can move forward without defeating the other — which has been a core reason for reform deadlock.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Led by Brazil and Germany, it proposes accelerated G4 admission to full permanent membership including veto power within two years
Led by the African Union, it proposes two new permanent seats for Africa with full veto power and no other category changes
Led by Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, and South Korea (each opposing a specific G4 rival), it proposes expansion only of non-permanent seats to 20 — opposing all new permanent memberships
Led by the P5 themselves, it proposes the complete freezing of the current Security Council composition with no expansion of any kind

elimination_hints.hint: Brazil and Germany are G4 members, not UfC members — they are on the opposite side of this reform debate; the UfC exists precisely to oppose the G4 permanent-seat demand.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse G4 (pro-permanent-seats) with UfC (anti-permanent-seats) — they are rival coalitions.

elimination_hints.hint: The African Union position is the Ezulwini Consensus — a separate proposal from UfC; the AU demands permanent seats with veto, while UfC opposes any new permanent seats — these are different proposals with different coalitions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the three main reform blocs — G4, Ezulwini Consensus (AU), and UfC.

elimination_hints.hint: The P5 have varied positions on reform — they are not a unified coalition; UfC is composed of middle powers opposing G4's specific rivals, not the P5 defending status quo.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overestimate P5 coordination on reform when they have different positions (US supportive of India/Japan, France/UK supportive of G4, China opposed to Japan, Russia cautious).

question_text: The procedural requirement for any UN Charter amendment is specified in Article 108 and represents a major structural obstacle to Security Council reform. What are the specific requirements, and why do they effectively give the P5 collective lock-in power over the current Council composition?
explanation: Article 108 of the UN Charter provides that amendments to the Charter shall come into force when adopted by a vote of two-thirds of the members of the General Assembly and ratified in accordance with their respective constitutional processes by two-thirds of the members of the United Nations, including all the permanent members of the Security Council. The 'including all the permanent members' clause is the critical part — it gives each of the five permanent members a veto over any Charter amendment, including amendments that would add new permanent members or reform the veto itself. This means that the P5 have collective lock-in power over their own position: no expansion, no veto reform, no change to the P5 composition can occur unless every single P5 member agrees to it. This is fundamentally different from ordinary Council votes where nine affirmative votes are needed — for Charter amendment, the P5 requirement is unanimous. Only one amendment (the 1963 expansion from 11 to 15 Council members) has ever been successfully enacted, and that required all P5 members to ratify. Since at least one P5 member has always had reasons to block any specific reform proposal (China opposes Japan, Russia opposes Germany in some framings, the US has been inconsistent), the procedural requirement effectively makes substantive reform impossible under current conditions.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Simple majority of the General Assembly is sufficient for Charter amendments, making reform procedurally easy — the main obstacles are political rather than procedural

Two-thirds of GA vote AND two-thirds ratification by member states INCLUDING all five P5 members — the P5 ratification requirement gives each P5 a veto over Charter amendment, locking in the current composition unless they all agree
Approval by the Secretary-General followed by a two-thirds vote in the General Assembly alone, without any Security Council involvement at all
Unanimous vote of the General Assembly (all 193 members) with no role for the Security Council or P5 ratification, reflecting sovereign equality principles

elimination_hints.hint: Simple majority is not enough — Article 108 requires two-thirds GA vote PLUS two-thirds state ratification INCLUDING all P5; the obstacles are both procedural (the P5 veto on amendment) and political.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underestimate the difficulty of amending the Charter by imagining simple majority rules when two-thirds plus P5 unanimity is required.

elimination_hints.hint: The Secretary-General has no role in Charter amendment; amendments require two-thirds GA vote plus two-thirds state ratification including all P5 — not SG approval followed by GA vote.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invent institutional procedures that do not exist when the actual Article 108 procedure is clearly specified.

elimination_hints.hint: Unanimity of all 193 members is not required — two-thirds is sufficient for both GA vote and state ratification; but the P5 'including all permanent members' clause gives the P5 collective veto, which is different from full unanimity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overstate the amendment difficulty to full unanimity when the actual rule is two-thirds plus P5 unanimity — still very difficult but not impossible procedurally.

question_text: Different P5 members have adopted different positions on UN Security Council reform, and this internal P5 division has shaped the reform trajectory. Which of the following most accurately characterises the positions of the five permanent members?
explanation: The five permanent members have adopted distinct positions reflecting their own strategic interests. The United Kingdom and France have publicly supported the G4 proposal, endorsing permanent seats for India, Brazil, Germany, and Japan — this reflects their European and Atlantic interests and also arguably strengthens their own legitimacy by expanding the Council rather than standing alone. The United States has been selectively supportive: President Obama explicitly endorsed India's permanent seat bid during his November 2010 visit, President Trump supported India and Japan individually, and the US has generally supported Japan's bid — but Washington has been hesitant about Germany (due to EU representation concerns) and ambivalent about Brazil. Russia has been diplomatically cautious, generally supportive in principle but not advocating strongly for any particular candidate, and wary of expansion that might dilute its own influence. China is the most restrictive — it actively opposes Japan's bid (reflecting historical tensions and regional rivalry) and is cautious about India (reflecting India-China border disputes and regional strategic competition), though Chinese public statements have been carefully calibrated to avoid outright opposition to reform in principle. This P5 division means that even if the G4 and UfC could agree on a proposal, getting unanimous P5 ratification under Article 108 faces obstacles from China's specific objections.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: All five P5 members are unanimously supportive of expanding the Council with new permanent members, and reform has been delayed only by logistical issues
All five P5 members are unanimously opposed to any expansion of the Council because expansion would dilute their influence — there is no P5 division on this question
The US is the only P5 opponent of reform; Russia, China, UK, and France all strongly support the G4 proposal without reservations

UK and France support G4; US supports India and Japan selectively; Russia is cautiously neutral; China actively opposes Japan and is cautious about India — this P5 division compounds the Article 108 lock-in problem

elimination_hints.hint: The P5 are not unanimous on reform — China actively opposes Japan, Russia is cautious, and US support has been selective and inconsistent; reform is blocked by political disagreement, not logistical issues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume P5 unity on reform when internal division is actually a key obstacle.

elimination_hints.hint: UK and France explicitly support the G4 proposal — they are not unified with other P5 members in opposing expansion; the US has selectively supported India and Japan; the P5 are genuinely divided.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students oversimplify P5 positions to uniform opposition when UK and France have been publicly supportive of G4 and US has been selectively supportive.

elimination_hints.hint: China does not support the G4 — it actively opposes Japan's bid; Russia is cautious rather than strongly supportive; UK and France support G4 but not Russia or China; US has been selective not opposed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invert the actual P5 positions by assuming US opposition and China/Russia support when the actual alignment is close to the reverse.

question_text: India has been elected to the UN Security Council as a non-permanent member multiple times, including its most recent term in 2021-22. What does this track record of non-permanent membership reveal about India's diplomatic position in the UN, and what is its relationship to India's permanent seat bid?
explanation: India has been elected to non-permanent Security Council seats eight times — in 1950-51, 1967-68, 1972-73, 1977-78, 1984-85, 1991-92, 2011-12, and most recently 2021-22. This makes India one of the most frequently elected non-permanent members, reflecting broad diplomatic acceptance across regional groupings. India's non-permanent election requires a two-thirds vote of the General Assembly (129 of 193 members), demonstrating substantial international support. The repeated elections show that India's candidature for the Council is legitimate and that large majorities of UN members are willing to trust India with Council responsibilities. However, non-permanent membership is not a stepping stone to permanent membership in any formal sense — there is no provision for automatic promotion. Rather, India's non-permanent service serves two functions in its permanent seat bid: first, it demonstrates capability and responsible behaviour that supports the case for permanent membership; second, it provides practical experience and diplomatic networks that strengthen India's voice in reform discussions. The frequent non-permanent elections also reinforce India's argument that its exclusion from permanent membership creates a visible representational gap — the UN majority clearly trusts India with Council responsibilities, yet the P5-locked structure prevents permanent elevation.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: India has never been elected as a non-permanent Council member because of P5 opposition, which demonstrates the complete diplomatic isolation of the Indian permanent seat bid

India has been elected eight times as non-permanent member (most recently 2021-22) through two-thirds GA votes, demonstrating broad diplomatic support; this strengthens India's permanent bid case but there is no automatic promotion from non-permanent to permanent membership
India has automatically become a permanent member through its repeated non-permanent elections, and the delay is only administrative in nature
India was expelled from the Council in 1992 and has been banned from seeking non-permanent membership since then, which is why the permanent bid is controversial

elimination_hints.hint: India has actually been elected eight times as non-permanent member — the most recent term was 2021-22 alongside the pandemic and Ukraine war debates; this is factually the opposite of 'never elected' and demonstrates broad, not isolated, support.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume permanent seat bid failure means isolation when India actually has broad diplomatic support for non-permanent roles.

elimination_hints.hint: There is no automatic promotion mechanism — non-permanent service does not convert into permanent status under any Charter provision; permanent status requires Article 108 amendment which has not occurred for Security Council expansion since 1963.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine promotion mechanisms that do not exist when the non-permanent and permanent categories are structurally separate under the Charter.

elimination_hints.hint: India has never been expelled from the Council — it was elected for 2011-12 and 2021-22 terms after 1992; this is factually fabricated; India's relationship with the UN has been continuous.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students invent fictional institutional events when the actual history is readily available.

question_text: The Intergovernmental Negotiations (IGN) process is the formal framework within which Security Council reform discussions have occurred since 2009. What does the IGN process consist of, and what does its lack of progress reveal about the realistic prospects for reform?
explanation: The Intergovernmental Negotiations on the Question of Equitable Representation on and Increase in the Membership of the Security Council (commonly called the IGN process) was formally launched in February 2009 by General Assembly Decision 62/557, and has been conducted through informal plenary meetings of the General Assembly addressing five key issues: categories of membership, the veto, regional representation, size and working methods, and the relationship between the Council and the General Assembly. Despite over fifteen years of negotiations, the IGN has consistently failed to produce any negotiating text that could serve as a basis for formal decision. The core obstacle is the UfC group's consistent refusal to accept any negotiating text that includes new permanent seats (which the G4 and African Union demand), while the G4 and African Union groups refuse to accept texts that exclude new permanent seats. Reform advocates including India have criticised the IGN for operating without a text-based negotiation framework — procedural delay has become a substantive obstacle. The lack of progress reveals several uncomfortable truths: there is no majority willing to force a vote on reform because that might produce a proposal unable to pass the Article 108 amendment hurdle; consensus-based negotiation allows UfC members (numerically smaller but determined) to block progress indefinitely; and P5 members have quietly preferred the deadlock because any reform would dilute their relative influence. Many observers now describe the IGN process as permanently stalled, with reform unlikely without a major external shock to the system.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: IGN is the formal GA framework for reform negotiation since 2009, addressing five key issues including categories and veto; despite 15+ years it has produced no negotiating text because UfC blocks any text with new permanent seats while G4 and AU block any text without — revealing that consensus negotiation allows determined minorities to indefinitely stall reform
IGN is a binding treaty process that produces legally enforceable Security Council decisions and has already achieved major reforms since 2009
IGN is a Security Council mechanism for reform discussions that excludes the General Assembly entirely and operates under P5 veto rules
IGN is an African-Union-led process that only considers the Ezulwini Consensus as the negotiating framework for regional representation

elimination_hints.hint: IGN is neither binding nor producing enforceable decisions — it is a GA consultation framework that has famously failed to produce even a negotiating text in over 15 years; no major reforms have been achieved through it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume any formal UN process must be producing results when the IGN is famous precisely for its lack of substantive progress.

elimination_hints.hint: IGN is a General Assembly framework, not a Security Council mechanism — it was launched by GA Decision 62/557; P5 veto rules do not apply directly to IGN negotiations though P5 members participate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse GA-level reform negotiations with Council-level veto procedures.

elimination_hints.hint: The IGN considers all reform proposals on their merits including G4, UfC, and Ezulwini Consensus — it is not AU-led nor limited to Ezulwini; the process deliberately addresses all viewpoints which is precisely why it cannot converge.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume one reform position dominates the IGN when the process is characterised by competing positions that cannot be reconciled.

question_text: Beyond Security Council composition reform, there are important debates about 'veto reform' that would limit P5 veto use in specific circumstances. Which of the following initiatives are the most significant attempts at veto reform, and what do their limited progress reveal?
explanation: Several major initiatives have attempted to limit P5 veto use in specific circumstances without requiring Charter amendment under Article 108. The French-Mexican Initiative (launched 2013 at UN General Debate) proposes a voluntary political commitment by P5 members to refrain from using the veto in situations involving mass atrocity crimes — genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes as defined in Article II of the Genocide Convention. It has been endorsed by 106 countries including France and the UK, but the US, Russia, and China have not signed on. The ACT Group (Accountability, Coherence, and Transparency) Code of Conduct, launched in 2015 by Liechtenstein with 27 founding members, similarly calls on all Security Council members (not just P5) to support credible action against mass atrocity crimes and not to vote against such action. It has over 120 endorsements as of 2023. The 'Veto Initiative' or 'Liechtenstein Resolution' adopted by the General Assembly in April 2022 (Resolution A/RES/76/262) requires an automatic GA debate within 10 working days whenever any P5 veto is cast, creating a modest accountability mechanism though without removing the veto. These initiatives represent the only realistic reform pathway — voluntary political commitments and accountability mechanisms within existing rules — because formal Charter amendment is blocked by the P5 themselves. Their limited progress reveals that even these modest voluntary mechanisms are rejected by key P5 members (US, Russia, China) who view any limitation on the veto as a threat to their strategic flexibility. Russia's use of the veto on Ukraine and the US use on Gaza have both prompted Liechtenstein Resolution GA debates but have not actually changed behaviour, confirming the deep structural resistance to veto reform.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Veto reform has been formally achieved through a Charter amendment in 2005 that eliminates veto power in mass atrocity situations and reduces P5 veto frequency
Veto reform is impossible because Article 108 prevents any modification of voting rules under any circumstances, so no attempts have been made

Three main initiatives — the French-Mexican Initiative, the ACT Code of Conduct, and the Liechtenstein Resolution (2022, requiring automatic GA debate on every veto) — attempt veto reform through voluntary commitments and accountability mechanisms rather than Charter amendment, but face resistance from US, Russia, and China
The only veto reform initiative is India's unilateral 2020 proposal, which has received no support from any other country or grouping

elimination_hints.hint: No Charter amendment on the veto has been achieved in 2005 or any other year — the only successful Security Council amendment was the 1963 expansion from 11 to 15 non-permanent seats; veto rules remain unchanged since 1945.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students overestimate reform achievements by imagining amendments that have not occurred.

elimination_hints.hint: Reform attempts have been made outside the Article 108 amendment route — through voluntary commitments and accountability mechanisms that do not require Charter change; the French-Mexican Initiative and ACT Code of Conduct are precisely this category of non-amendment reform.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume all reform must occur through formal Charter amendment when voluntary commitments have been the main reform pathway.

elimination_hints.hint: The main veto reform initiatives are the French-Mexican Initiative (106+ endorsements), ACT Code of Conduct (120+ endorsements), and Liechtenstein Resolution (adopted by GA); India has not proposed a named unilateral veto reform initiative.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students imagine India-led initiatives when the main veto reform efforts have come from France, Mexico, Liechtenstein, and small-state coalitions.

question_text:
Match the following UN reform proposals in Column A with their correct main sponsors or demand in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) G4 Proposal | (P) African Union's unified position demanding two permanent seats with full veto power plus two non-permanent seats for Africa |
| (ii) Ezulwini Consensus | (Q) Brazil, Germany, India, Japan demanding permanent seats through mutual endorsement, accepting 15-year veto suspension transition |
| (iii) Uniting for Consensus | (R) Led by Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, South Korea — proposing expansion only of non-permanent seats, opposing new permanent memberships |
| (iv) Razali Plan | (S) 1997 reform proposal by GA President Ismail Razali of Malaysia proposing new permanent seats without veto power and new non-permanent seats |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: African Union's unified position demanding two permanent seats with full veto power plus two non-permanent seats for Africa
Brazil, Germany, India, Japan demanding permanent seats through mutual endorsement, accepting 15-year veto suspension transition
Led by Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, South Korea — proposing expansion only of non-permanent seats, opposing new permanent memberships
1997 reform proposal by GA President Ismail Razali of Malaysia proposing new permanent seats without veto power and new non-permanent seats

explanation: The G4 Proposal (i) is Brazil, Germany, India, Japan demanding permanent seats through mutual endorsement with a 15-year veto transition (Q). The Ezulwini Consensus (ii) is the African Union's unified position demanding two permanent seats with full veto power plus two additional non-permanent seats for Africa (P). Uniting for Consensus (iii) is led by Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, South Korea and proposes expanding only non-permanent seats (R). The Razali Plan (iv) was a 1997 proposal by GA President Ismail Razali of Malaysia offering new permanent seats without veto and new non-permanent seats (S).
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-R, ii-P, iii-Q, iv-S
i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes the Ezulwini Consensus (AU position), not G4; G4 is the Brazil-Germany-India-Japan grouping (Q), not an African Union position.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse G4 with the African Union Ezulwini Consensus, both being major reform proposals.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes the Uniting for Consensus group (UfC), not G4; G4 demands new permanent seats (Q), while UfC opposes new permanent seats — they are direct rivals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse G4 (pro-permanent) and UfC (anti-permanent) which are opposite proposals.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes UfC (opposing permanent seats), not Ezulwini; the Ezulwini Consensus actually demands permanent seats with veto (P), the opposite of UfC's position.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse Ezulwini (pro-permanent with veto) and UfC (anti-permanent) which are opposite proposals.

question_text:
Match the following countries and their reform alignment in Column A with their reasons for that alignment in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) Pakistan in UfC | (P) Opposes permanent seat for regional rival (Japan) due to historical grievances over WWII and contemporary East Asian strategic competition |
| (ii) Italy in UfC | (Q) Opposes permanent seat for regional rival (India) due to bilateral conflict and the implications of Indian Council elevation for Kashmir disputes |
| (iii) China opposing Japan | (R) Opposes permanent seat for Germany because it would give Germany Council parity despite France and UK already holding EU seats |
| (iv) Argentina in UfC | (S) Opposes permanent seat for regional rival (Brazil) based on historical Latin American rivalry and concerns about Brazilian regional dominance |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Opposes permanent seat for regional rival (Japan) due to historical grievances over WWII and contemporary East Asian strategic competition
Opposes permanent seat for regional rival (India) due to bilateral conflict and the implications of Indian Council elevation for Kashmir disputes
Opposes permanent seat for Germany because it would give Germany Council parity despite France and UK already holding EU seats
Opposes permanent seat for regional rival (Brazil) based on historical Latin American rivalry and concerns about Brazilian regional dominance

explanation: Pakistan's UfC membership (i) is driven by opposition to India's permanent seat bid because of bilateral conflict and Kashmir implications (Q). Italy's UfC role (ii) reflects its opposition to Germany's permanent seat since France and UK already represent Europe (R). China's opposition to Japan (iii) stems from WWII historical grievances and contemporary East Asian strategic competition (P). Argentina's UfC membership (iv) reflects opposition to Brazilian regional dominance within Latin America (S). This pattern reveals that most reform opposition is driven by bilateral regional rivalry rather than principled institutional preference — each UfC member has a specific G4 rival they want to block.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: i-Q, ii-R, iii-P, iv-S
i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-R, ii-P, iii-Q, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: P describes China-Japan rivalry (WWII grievances, East Asian competition), not Pakistan-India; Pakistan's opposition is based on Kashmir and bilateral conflict with India (Q), not WWII historical issues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the different bilateral rivalries underlying UfC membership — each member has a specific regional rival to block.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes Italy-Germany rivalry (EU representation concerns), not Pakistan-India; Pakistan's opposition to India is about bilateral conflict and Kashmir (Q), not European Union parity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students mix up the distinct regional logics behind different UfC members' opposition.

elimination_hints.hint: P describes China-Japan rivalry (WWII, East Asian competition), not Italy-Germany; Italy's opposition to Germany concerns EU representation and the position that France and UK already represent Europe (R).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the East Asian (China-Japan) and European (Italy-Germany) reform rivalries.

question_text:
Match the following analytical frameworks for understanding UN reform deadlock in Column A with their correct descriptions or proponents in Column B:

| Column A | Column B |
|----------|----------|
| (i) 'Principal-agent problem' | (P) Inefficiency arising because the agents (P5) entrusted with collective security have interests that diverge from the principal (international community) and face no real accountability mechanism |
| (ii) 'Joint decision trap' | (Q) Fritz Scharpf's concept — decision-making systems requiring supermajority or unanimity become unable to adapt because changing the rules itself requires using the same blocked rules |
| (iii) 'Lock-in effect' | (R) Institutional structures created at one moment get frozen despite changed conditions because the winners at the founding moment retain veto power over changes |
| (iv) 'Path dependence' | (S) Choices made at critical moments shape subsequent possibilities because accumulated investment, adaptation, and expectation make reversal increasingly costly |

Choose the correct matching:

column_b.text: Inefficiency arising because the agents (P5) entrusted with collective security have interests that diverge from the principal (international community) and face no real accountability mechanism
Fritz Scharpf's concept — decision-making systems requiring supermajority or unanimity become unable to adapt because changing the rules itself requires using the same blocked rules
Institutional structures created at one moment get frozen despite changed conditions because the winners at the founding moment retain veto power over changes
Choices made at critical moments shape subsequent possibilities because accumulated investment, adaptation, and expectation make reversal increasingly costly

explanation: The principal-agent problem (i) describes how the P5 as 'agents' of collective security have interests diverging from the 'principal' (international community) with no accountability mechanism (P). The joint decision trap (ii) is Fritz Scharpf's classic concept describing how supermajority/unanimity requirements prevent rule changes that would themselves require using the blocked rules (Q) — this exactly describes the Article 108 amendment problem. The lock-in effect (iii) describes how institutional founders retain veto power over changes, freezing structures despite changed conditions (R). Path dependence (iv) is a broader concept where early choices shape later possibilities through accumulated investment and adaptation costs (S). These four frameworks, all drawn from comparative political economy and international institution theory, provide rigorous analytical vocabulary for understanding why UN reform has been so stubbornly blocked despite widespread recognition of the need for change.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: i-P, ii-Q, iii-R, iv-S
i-Q, ii-P, iii-R, iv-S
i-P, ii-R, iii-Q, iv-S
i-R, ii-Q, iii-P, iv-S

elimination_hints.hint: Q describes the joint decision trap (Scharpf), not the principal-agent problem; principal-agent is specifically about divergence between delegating principal and acting agent (P), which describes the international community vs P5 relationship.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the principal-agent framework with the joint decision trap framework — both relate to institutional failure but through different mechanisms.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes the lock-in effect (institutional founders retaining veto power), not the joint decision trap; the joint decision trap is Fritz Scharpf's specific concept about supermajority/unanimity rules becoming unchangeable (Q).
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse lock-in and joint decision trap frameworks — both explain institutional rigidity but through different causal mechanisms.

elimination_hints.hint: R describes the lock-in effect (founding veto power), not the principal-agent problem; the principal-agent problem is about agency accountability (P), which is conceptually different from founding lock-in.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students confuse the accountability framework (principal-agent) with the rigidity framework (lock-in).

assertion: India's case for a permanent UN Security Council seat is widely recognised as substantively strong on multiple criteria simultaneously — demographic, democratic, economic, and contribution-based — yet India has failed to secure this seat despite decades of diplomatic effort because the obstacle is not the strength of its case but the structural features of UN reform procedure.
reason: The UN Charter Article 108 amendment procedure requires two-thirds GA approval plus ratification by two-thirds of member states including all five P5 members, meaning that any single P5 member can veto Charter amendment creating new permanent seats, and China's ambiguous position on India's bid combined with the unresolved G4 vs UfC deadlock within the General Assembly makes reaching the amendment threshold practically impossible regardless of how strong India's substantive case is.
question_text:
Assertion (A): India's case for a permanent UN Security Council seat is widely recognised as substantively strong on multiple criteria simultaneously — demographic, democratic, economic, and contribution-based — yet India has failed to secure this seat despite decades of diplomatic effort because the obstacle is not the strength of its case but the structural features of UN reform procedure.
Reason (R): The UN Charter Article 108 amendment procedure requires two-thirds GA approval plus ratification by two-thirds of member states including all five P5 members, meaning that any single P5 member can veto Charter amendment creating new permanent seats, and China's ambiguous position on India's bid combined with the unresolved G4 vs UfC deadlock within the General Assembly makes reaching the amendment threshold practically impossible regardless of how strong India's substantive case is.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. R identifies the specific procedural mechanisms — Article 108 requirements, China's position, GA deadlock — that directly explain A's claim that substantive merit is not the obstacle. The logic is that reform failure is not about India's case being weak but about the structural design of the reform procedure itself. This is the standard framing in Indian foreign policy scholarship and Indian diplomatic rhetoric — India has made substantial investment in making its case and the failure is attributed to procedural architecture rather than substantive rejection of Indian claims.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies precisely the procedural mechanisms (Article 108, China, deadlock) that directly explain A's claim that substantive merit is not the obstacle; these are not parallel observations but explanation and conclusion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's observation about substantive merit and R's procedural explanation as independent when R is precisely the causal logic for A's situation.

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually accurate — Article 108 does require two-thirds GA approval plus two-thirds ratification including all P5 members; China has been publicly ambiguous about India's bid; the G4 vs UfC deadlock is well-documented in IGN negotiations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question R's procedural and political facts when they are straightforwardly accurate institutional features.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by mainstream international relations scholarship — India's case on demographic, democratic, economic, and peacekeeping contribution criteria is widely acknowledged; the case's strength is rarely the point of contention in reform debates.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students dismiss A's claim about India's substantive case when this is broadly accepted even by critics of the permanent seat bid.

assertion: The decades-long failure of Security Council reform has paradoxically strengthened the position of the existing P5 members, even as the legitimacy of the Council as a whole has diminished — creating a dangerous divergence between formal institutional authority and substantive international acceptance.
reason: Each year that reform fails deepens the entrenchment of the existing P5 as the de facto decision-makers of global peace and security, while their collective ability to block any reform through Article 108 increases P5 bargaining power over the General Assembly; at the same time, the Council's legitimacy erosion has pushed important peace and security debates into alternative venues (G20, BRICS, regional organisations) where the P5's privileged status does not apply.
question_text:
Assertion (A): The decades-long failure of Security Council reform has paradoxically strengthened the position of the existing P5 members, even as the legitimacy of the Council as a whole has diminished — creating a dangerous divergence between formal institutional authority and substantive international acceptance.
Reason (R): Each year that reform fails deepens the entrenchment of the existing P5 as the de facto decision-makers of global peace and security, while their collective ability to block any reform through Article 108 increases P5 bargaining power over the General Assembly; at the same time, the Council's legitimacy erosion has pushed important peace and security debates into alternative venues (G20, BRICS, regional organisations) where the P5's privileged status does not apply.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. R identifies two specific mechanisms — P5 entrenchment through amendment veto leverage, and legitimacy erosion driving issues to alternative venues — that together explain A's paradox of simultaneous P5 strengthening and Council weakening. This is a sophisticated framing found in international institutions scholarship (notably by Ian Hurd on legitimacy, Alexander Thompson on institutional inertia, and Amitav Acharya on global governance fragmentation). The paradox points to a dangerous dynamic: the Council becomes increasingly important formally (binding Chapter VII resolutions) while becoming increasingly bypassed substantively (major issues discussed in G20, regional bodies, ad hoc coalitions), producing a legitimacy-authority gap that threatens the entire post-1945 security architecture.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies the two specific mechanisms — P5 entrenchment through amendment leverage, and legitimacy erosion pushing issues to alternative venues — that together explain A's paradox of simultaneous strengthening and weakening.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's paradox and R's dual mechanism as parallel observations when R provides precisely the causal explanation for how the paradox operates.

elimination_hints.hint: R is supported by empirical observation — major global economic discussions have moved to the G20, BRICS has become a platform for alternative governance, regional organisations handle many peace and security discussions; P5 control over amendment is textually supported by Article 108.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question R's observations about institutional venue fragmentation when this is a well-documented feature of 21st century global governance.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by international institutions scholarship — the paradox of increasing P5 formal power combined with Council legitimacy erosion is a standard finding in work by Ian Hurd, Alexander Thompson, and others on UN legitimacy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students dismiss A's paradox as overstated when it reflects a mainstream analytical framing in international institutions scholarship.

assertion: Even if India were admitted as a permanent member of the Security Council without veto power (as the G4 proposal originally envisaged), this outcome would represent only a partial victory because the structural source of Council paralysis — the P5 veto — would remain unchanged, meaning India would gain status and voice but not actual decisional power over blocked issues.
reason: The veto is the operative source of Council paralysis on contested issues — veto-less permanent membership would give India speaking rights, agenda-setting leverage, and institutional presence but would not allow India to force action on issues where existing P5 members use their veto; India's actual capacity to shape outcomes on issues like Syria, Ukraine, or Gaza would be no different from its current non-permanent member participation.
question_text:
Assertion (A): Even if India were admitted as a permanent member of the Security Council without veto power (as the G4 proposal originally envisaged), this outcome would represent only a partial victory because the structural source of Council paralysis — the P5 veto — would remain unchanged, meaning India would gain status and voice but not actual decisional power over blocked issues.
Reason (R): The veto is the operative source of Council paralysis on contested issues — veto-less permanent membership would give India speaking rights, agenda-setting leverage, and institutional presence but would not allow India to force action on issues where existing P5 members use their veto; India's actual capacity to shape outcomes on issues like Syria, Ukraine, or Gaza would be no different from its current non-permanent member participation.
explanation: Both A and R are analytically sound and causally connected. R identifies the specific mechanism — veto as the operative paralysis source — that directly explains A's 'partial victory' claim. The logic is that permanent membership without veto gives status and continuity but not decisional override, meaning India would have more symbolic presence but the same substantive limitations on contested issues. This is why some scholars and India's own position have evolved to emphasise that 'any reform must include the veto' — Ezulwini Consensus goes further, demanding veto power for new permanent members precisely because veto-less membership is seen as inadequate. The assertion reveals the tension within India's position: publicly supporting G4's veto-less permanent seat proposal as a practical compromise, while recognising that full veto parity would be the outcome that actually changes India's decisional power. This analytical distinction is essential for understanding why reform is both politically difficult and strategically complex.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true, but R is false
A is false, but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R identifies precisely the operative role of the veto as the paralysis source that explains A's 'partial victory' claim about veto-less membership; these link as causal mechanism and conclusion, not as parallel observations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students see A's claim and R's mechanism as independent when R is precisely the explanation for why A's partial victory framing is correct.

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually accurate — the veto is indeed the operative source of Council paralysis on contested issues (Syria, Ukraine, Gaza); veto-less permanent membership would give additional institutional weight but not decisional override, which is a standard observation in reform scholarship.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students question R's observation about the veto's operative role when this is directly observable in Council voting records.

elimination_hints.hint: A is supported by serious analysis — the G4 veto-suspension proposal was a political compromise, not a substantive optimum; Ezulwini Consensus demands full veto precisely because veto-less permanent membership is seen as inadequate for actual decisional power.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume any permanent membership would be full victory when the substantive analysis recognises the gap between permanent seats with and without veto.

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the correct chronological sequence showing the evolution of UN Security Council reform discussions:

(i) Razali Plan presented by GA President Ismail Razali of Malaysia as the first comprehensive reform proposal
(ii) G4 Proposal formalised by Brazil, Germany, India, Japan with mutual endorsement for permanent seats
(iii) Ezulwini Consensus adopted by the African Union as the continental common position on UN reform
(iv) Intergovernmental Negotiations (IGN) formally launched by the General Assembly to consider reform options

items.text: Razali Plan presented by GA President Ismail Razali of Malaysia as the first comprehensive reform proposal
G4 Proposal formalised by Brazil, Germany, India, Japan with mutual endorsement for permanent seats
Ezulwini Consensus adopted by the African Union as the continental common position on UN reform
Intergovernmental Negotiations (IGN) formally launched by the General Assembly to consider reform options

explanation: The Razali Plan was presented in March 1997 by Ismail Razali of Malaysia as GA President, making it the first comprehensive reform proposal (i). The G4 Proposal was formalised in 2004-2005 when Brazil, Germany, India, and Japan jointly presented their mutual endorsement for permanent seats (ii). The Ezulwini Consensus was adopted in March 2005 by the African Union at its Executive Council meeting in Addis Ababa (iii). The Intergovernmental Negotiations (IGN) process was formally launched in February 2009 by GA Decision 62/557 (iv). This sequence traces the major reform moments from the late 1990s through the mid-2000s proposal phase to the current stalled negotiation framework.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: i → ii → iii → iv
ii → i → iii → iv
i → iii → ii → iv
ii → iii → i → iv

elimination_hints.hint: The Razali Plan (i, 1997) came before the G4 Proposal (ii, 2004-05) — Razali was the early reform template, the G4 emerged later building on accumulated discussions from the late 1990s.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the more famous G4 Proposal before the less known Razali Plan, missing the earlier reform period.

elimination_hints.hint: The G4 Proposal (ii, 2004-05) was formalised slightly before the Ezulwini Consensus (iii, March 2005) — G4 actually prompted the AU to formalise its own position in response.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students reverse the G4 and Ezulwini timing, not recognising that G4 emerged first and catalysed the AU's formal position.

elimination_hints.hint: The Razali Plan (i, 1997) was the earliest major reform event — before G4 (ii, 2004-05), Ezulwini (iii, 2005), and IGN (iv, 2009); this option places the earliest event third.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underestimate how early formal reform discussions began, placing the Razali Plan after later developments.

question_text:
Arrange the following developments in the correct chronological sequence showing India's engagement with the Security Council and its permanent seat bid:

(i) India elected to its first non-permanent Security Council term during the early Cold War period
(ii) India signs India-US Civil Nuclear Cooperation agreement — part of broader US recognition of India's rise as a responsible power
(iii) President Obama publicly endorses India's permanent UNSC seat bid during his state visit to India
(iv) India completes its eighth non-permanent Security Council term, during which it holds the Council presidency twice and engages with Ukraine war debates

items.text: India elected to its first non-permanent Security Council term during the early Cold War period
India signs India-US Civil Nuclear Cooperation agreement — part of broader US recognition of India's rise as a responsible power
President Obama publicly endorses India's permanent UNSC seat bid during his state visit to India
India completes its eighth non-permanent Security Council term, during which it holds the Council presidency twice and engages with Ukraine war debates

explanation: India served its first non-permanent Council term in 1950-51 during the early Cold War (i). The India-US Civil Nuclear Cooperation Agreement was signed in 2008 (negotiation concluded in 2007, IAEA safeguards approved in 2008) (ii). President Obama publicly endorsed India's permanent UNSC seat bid during his state visit in November 2010 (iii). India completed its eighth non-permanent term at the end of 2022 (2021-22 term), during which India held the rotating Council presidency in August 2021 and December 2022 and engaged actively with the Ukraine war debates from February 2022 onwards (iv). This sequence traces India's gradual elevation from an early post-independence Council member to a modern major power with explicit US endorsement and recent active engagement.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: i → ii → iii → iv
ii → i → iii → iv
i → iii → ii → iv
ii → iii → i → iv

elimination_hints.hint: India's first non-permanent term (i, 1950-51) came long before the Civil Nuclear Agreement (ii, 2008) — India was engaging with the Council from its earliest UN participation, over five decades before the nuclear deal.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume India's UN engagement began more recently, missing the fact that India has been a Council participant since 1950.

elimination_hints.hint: The Civil Nuclear Agreement (ii, 2008) came before Obama's endorsement (iii, 2010) — the nuclear deal was a precondition for the subsequent diplomatic upgrading that produced the UNSC endorsement, not the other way around.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students place the Obama endorsement before the nuclear deal when the deal was the crucial enabling step for the subsequent endorsement.

elimination_hints.hint: India's first non-permanent term (i, 1950-51) was the earliest event — before the nuclear deal (ii, 2008) and Obama endorsement (iii, 2010); this option places the earliest event third.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students underestimate how early India began its Council engagement, placing the first term after much later developments.

question_text: India, Brazil, and South Africa are founding members of the G4 group that jointly demands permanent Security Council seats through mutual diplomatic endorsement as their core reform strategy.
explanation: This statement is false. The G4 consists of Brazil, Germany, India, and Japan — not South Africa. South Africa is not a G4 member. South Africa instead participates in the Ezulwini Consensus framework through the African Union, where it is one of the candidates for an African permanent seat alongside Nigeria, Egypt, and Kenya (though the AU has not formally nominated specific candidates). The confusion may arise because India, Brazil, and South Africa form the IBSA grouping — a trilateral dialogue forum established in 2003 for cooperation among three major democracies of the Global South — but IBSA is not the G4 and is not specifically a UN reform coalition. All three IBSA members do favour UN reform including new permanent seats, but their institutional affiliation is different: India and Brazil are G4 members while South Africa is part of the African Union's Ezulwini process. The false statement confuses the separate IBSA and G4 groupings, which share some overlapping membership but have different purposes and compositions.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: True
False

question_text:
Study the following comparative table showing the major UN Security Council reform proposals and their core features:

| Proposal | Main Sponsors | New Permanent Seats | Veto for New Members | Total Council Size | Main Opposition |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| G4 Proposal | Brazil, Germany, India, Japan | 6 (4 for G4 + 2 Africa) | No — 15-year suspension then review | ~25 members | UfC group and China |
| Ezulwini Consensus | African Union (54 states) | 2 (both for Africa) plus 2 non-permanent | Yes — full veto from day one | ~26 members | G4 (veto demand) and UfC (all permanent seats) |
| Uniting for Consensus | Italy, Pakistan, Argentina, South Korea et al | 0 (no new permanent seats) | N/A (no new permanent seats) | ~25 members (non-permanent only) | G4 and African Union |
| Razali Plan (1997) | GA President Ismail Razali | 5 (India, Germany, Japan, Brazil, Africa) | No | 24 members | Never formally adopted |
| Status Quo | P5 members (de facto) | 0 | N/A | 15 members (unchanged since 1963) | G4, AU, and reform advocates |

Based on the data in the table, which of the following conclusions is MOST accurately supported about the current state of UN Security Council reform?
explanation: The table reveals several important features of the reform deadlock. First, the proposals are fundamentally incompatible with each other on the core question of new permanent seats: G4 demands 6 new seats without veto, Ezulwini demands 2 new seats with full veto, and UfC demands 0 new permanent seats. No intermediate position can satisfy all three groups simultaneously. Second, the veto question divides even the pro-permanent-seat groups: G4 accepts veto suspension, Ezulwini insists on full veto — making these two allies on principle but rivals on specifics. Third, even the most modest proposals (Razali Plan, 1997) have never been formally adopted. Fourth, the status quo coalition (effectively the P5 protecting their position through Article 108 amendment requirements) has substantive 'voting power' in any reform that requires Charter amendment. The structural logic is that each proposal has a group that would block it under Article 108, producing the persistent deadlock since 1997. This is not primarily a problem of insufficient consensus-seeking but of genuinely incompatible preferences backed by procedural veto power.
topic: UN Reforms and India's UNSC Bid
subject: political-science

options.text: The reform proposals are very similar to each other and reform has been delayed only by minor technical differences that could be resolved through additional negotiation time
Only the Razali Plan has been officially adopted as UN policy, and all other proposals have been formally rejected by the General Assembly
The status quo with P5 unchanged since 1945 reflects broad consensus that the Council is functioning well and no reform is needed

The proposals are fundamentally incompatible — G4 demands new permanent seats without veto, Ezulwini demands permanent seats with veto, UfC demands no new permanent seats — and each proposal has a group that would block it under Article 108 amendment procedure, producing persistent deadlock regardless of negotiating effort

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows the proposals differ on core fundamental questions — number of new permanent seats (0 vs 2 vs 6), veto for new members (yes vs no) — these are not minor technical differences but fundamental incompatibilities that cannot be resolved through additional negotiation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misread the table's quantitative gaps as minor when the differences are qualitative and fundamental.

elimination_hints.hint: The table explicitly shows the Razali Plan 'never formally adopted' — and no reform proposal has been officially adopted; the General Assembly has not formally rejected the G4, Ezulwini, or UfC proposals either — they remain under discussion in the IGN process.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students assume formal adoption or rejection when the actual situation is persistent informal stalemate without formal disposition.

elimination_hints.hint: The table shows the 'Main Opposition' row for Status Quo includes G4, AU, and reform advocates — demonstrating broad opposition to the unchanged composition; this contradicts the claim of 'broad consensus that the Council is functioning well'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Students misread the persistent status quo as evidence of satisfaction when it is evidence of procedural deadlock despite broad dissatisfaction.
